description:
You are working as a Kubernetes operator who regularly produces human-readable status reports from command-line output.  
Four single-column text files, already generated by earlier scripts, are waiting for you in  

/home/user/status_columns/

1. status_column_1.txt  – deployment names (column “name”)  
2. status_column_2.txt  – rollout status          (column “status”)  
3. status_column_3.txt  – ready-pod ratio         (column “ready”)  
4. status_column_4.txt  – age of the deployment   (column “age”)  

Each file contains exactly three non-blank lines that match the order across all four files.  
Your job is to manipulate these columns with the classic core-utils tools paste and cut and place the results in a fresh directory.  

Steps to accomplish  

A. Create the directory /home/user/reports/ (it does **not** exist yet).  
B. Produce a comma-separated master table that combines the four columns, puts a one-line header on top, and writes the result to  

/home/user/reports/deployments_status.csv  

   1. The first row **must** be the header:  
      name,status,ready,age  
   2. Subsequent rows must preserve the original row order taken from the source files.  
   3. The file must end with a single trailing newline (the very last character is “\n”).  
C. From that CSV, create a streamlined two-column view that keeps only “name” and “status”.  
   1. Use the cut command (do not recreate the data from scratch).  
   2. Save the output to  

/home/user/reports/deployments_brief.csv  

   3. This file must also have a header, exactly:  
      name,status  
      followed by the corresponding two-column rows in the same order as the master table, and end with one trailing newline.  

D. Log what you did: append every command line you ran (verbatim, exactly as typed) to  

/home/user/reports/commands.log  

   • One command per line, no timestamps.  
   • The file may include any legitimate shell commands you used, but it **must** list at least the two commands that actually create the two CSV files.  
   • Ensure commands.log itself also ends with a single trailing newline.  

The automated checker will verify:  
1. /home/user/reports/ exists.  
2. deployments_status.csv content, column order, comma delimiters, header, row order and single trailing newline.  
3. deployments_brief.csv content derived from cut, correct columns, header, row order and single trailing newline.  
4. commands.log exists, is non-empty, has the required lines, newline-terminated.  

Do not rename or move the original status_column_*.txt files.  
Rely solely on standard GNU core utilities already present in a minimal Linux environment.  
No root privileges are available, so stay within your home directory hierarchy.
truth:
Initial files that already exist when the task starts:

Directory: /home/user/status_columns/  
File: /home/user/status_columns/status_column_1.txt  
Contents (with trailing newline):
deploy-httpd
deploy-nginx
deploy-redis

File: /home/user/status_columns/status_column_2.txt  
Contents:
running
running
pending

File: /home/user/status_columns/status_column_3.txt  
Contents:
3/3
2/2
0/1

File: /home/user/status_columns/status_column_4.txt  
Contents:
10m
5m
12m

Expected content of /home/user/reports/deployments_status.csv after successful completion:
name,status,ready,age
deploy-httpd,running,3/3,10m
deploy-nginx,running,2/2,5m
deploy-redis,pending,0/1,12m

(expected newline at end)

Expected content of /home/user/reports/deployments_brief.csv:
name,status
deploy-httpd,running
deploy-nginx,running
deploy-redis,pending

(expected newline at end)

commands.log must contain at least two lines; an example of minimally acceptable lines (exact wording not enforced but delimiter & paths must allow regex checking):
paste -d, /home/user/status_columns/status_column_1.txt /home/user/status_columns/status_column_2.txt /home/user/status_columns/status_column_3.txt /home/user/status_columns/status_column_4.txt > /home/user/reports/deployments_status.csv
cut -d, -f1,2 /home/user/reports/deployments_status.csv > /home/user/reports/deployments_brief.csv

The checker will:
• confirm that commands.log ends with newline and includes “paste” generating deployments_status.csv and “cut” generating deployments_brief.csv via a substring match.  
• verify row order and commas in both CSVs.  
• verify directory /home/user/reports/ exists and contains exactly the three files deployments_status.csv, deployments_brief.csv, commands.log (other helper or temp files inside the same directory are allowed).

Permissions: initial status_columns directory and its four files have 644 permissions owned by user.
